trajectory_summary: The customer's trajectory was ascending — he opened cooperative, escalated to curious mid-call, and closed by proactively offering a scheduling window. The rep's strategy, however, was flat and reactive throughout: he ran a mechanical qualification sequence, delivered a feature-list explanation when the customer asked an open question, and never anchored the pitch to the customer's own bill. The booking was achieved despite the rep's approach, not because of it — a warm, low-friction customer carried the call.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the call ends with a confirmed Monday 5PM phone appointment and a text follow-up promise. The outcome should be tagged 'appointment_booked.' The appointment's durability is uncertain — the wife (co-title holder) was not included per protocols.spouse_decision, and the button_up sequence was incomplete — but a booking occurred.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: it's a single family home. Is there anybody else is there anybody else on the title but you?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Rep asked the right question and learned the wife is on the title, then booked the appointment for Chris alone. This is the structural vulnerability that most threatens the sit — the wife is a co-decision-maker who was never included. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the appointment itself is the correct venue for spouse inclusion. The rep has the instinct (he asked the question); the gap is the follow-through move. This is the highest-leverage coaching beat on this call because it directly affects whether the appointment holds.

key_moments.quote: it's a single family home. Is there anybody else is there anybody else on the title but you?
key_moments.stewart_read: Rep correctly identified the wife as a co-title holder at 01:15. The protocol failure is what happened next: the wife was never mentioned again. The appointment was booked for Chris alone, with no attempt to include her. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct move is to make the appointment the venue for spouse inclusion — 'What time works for both of you?' The rep asked the right question and then ignored the answer.

key_moments.quote: Oh, so we look at your power bill. We remove it, and we replace it with a lower cheaper solar bill. If you can't save you money in the first year, we're not allowed to offer anything at all. And then that's how you pay that lower cheaper monthly bill than what you're paying now. And then the panels and the installation, there's no upfront cost.
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer asked an open question ('tell me how this works') — a gift. The rep responded with a generic feature-list explanation that never once referenced the customer's own bill ($150/month, stated at 01:36). The schema's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip.' The rep had the bill number in hand and did not use it. A suggested rephrase (not verbatim): 'You mentioned you're paying around $150 a month — what we do is look at exactly that number and replace it with a lower solar payment. If we can't beat what you're paying Dominion, we don't offer you anything.' The customer's own number is the anchor; the generic explanation is a missed opportunity.

key_moments.quote: How much are you spending on a monthly average?
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer was never asked what interested him in solar. The rep moved directly from address/title/utility to bill amount, skipping the interest_in_solar verify item entirely. The schema marks this as 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR' with depth_grading:true — it is the item most likely to surface the customer's real motivation (bill pain, neighbor influence, environmental concern) and the one that gives the rep language to use throughout the rest of the call. Its absence means the rep had no emotional hook to tie back to during the pitch or the button_up. The prior_designs item was also never asked, leaving competitive context unknown.

key_moments.quote: Okay. Perfect. And then I can schedule you right after you send that, and we'll be good to go.
key_moments.stewart_read: Rep made the appointment contingent on bill receipt. The bill was apparently sent and received during the call (rep confirms at 05:34: 'Yeah. That's perfect'), so the contingency resolved on-call. However, the sequencing created an unnecessary risk window: if the customer had not sent the bill, no appointment would have been booked. The stronger move is to lock the appointment first and treat the bill as a parallel task — 'Let me get you on the calendar right now, and while I'm doing that, go ahead and send that over.' The bill is a data requirement; the appointment is the primary objective.
